### 136. Lovaas Model of Applied Behavior Analysis

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/295"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, learning-engineering, wwc]
```

WWC Intervention Report on the Lovaas Model of Applied Behavior Analysis, an intensive behavioral therapy for PreK children with disabilities delivering 35–40 hours weekly of one-on-one discrete trial instruction — brief periods where a teacher cues a behavior, prompts the appropriate response, and provides reinforcement — for approximately 3 years. Reviews 2 studies meeting standards out of 8 eligible. Found potentially positive effects on cognitive development and no discernible effects on communication/language, social-emotional development, or functional abilities.

---

### 137. Accelerated Middle Schools

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/11"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, dropout-prevention, learning-engineering, wwc]
```

WWC Intervention Report on Accelerated Middle Schools, self-contained academic programs for grades 6–8 students behind grade level enabling them to progress an additional year of curriculum within 1–2 years to keep them on track through high school. Reviews 3 studies meeting standards. Found positive effects on progressing in school and staying in school (predominantly Black, urban student population).

---

### 138. Effective Teacher Professional Development

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/effective-teacher-professional-development"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, learning-engineering, lpi]
```

Learning Policy Institute research synthesis by Darling-Hammond, Hyler, and Gardner (2017) identifying seven features of effective teacher professional development across 35 rigorous studies. Finds effective PD is content-focused, incorporates active learning, supports collaboration, models effective practice, provides coaching and expert support, offers feedback and reflection, and is sustained over time. Concludes that PD meeting these criteria demonstrably improves teacher practice and student learning outcomes.

---

### 139. Design Principles for Teacher Preparation: Enacting the Science of Learning and Development

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/sold-design-principles-teacher-preparation-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, learning-engineering, lpi, higher-ed]
```

Learning Policy Institute report by Darling-Hammond, Hyler, Wojcikiewicz, and Rushing (2025) presenting five evidence-based design principles for teacher preparation programs grounded in the science of learning and development. Principles address: curriculum grounded in developmental understanding of learners; equitable educator mindsets and skills; rich experiential learning with feedback cycles; pedagogical alignment between teacher educators and candidates; and supportive developmental relationships within programs and PreK–12 school partnerships. Draws on neuroscience, developmental psychology, and exemplary program practices.

---

### 140. Community Schools Impact on Student Outcomes: Evidence From California

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/ca-community-schools-impact-student-outcomes-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, lpi, attendance, sel]
```

Learning Policy Institute study by Swain, Leung-Gagné, Maier, and Rubinstein (2025) evaluating California's Community Schools Partnership Program (CCSPP), a $4.1 billion state initiative. Analyzes 458 schools using matched difference-in-differences methodology across 2018–19 through 2023–24. Finds 30% greater reduction in chronic absenteeism, ~15% decline in suspensions, and academic gains of 0.06 SD in math and 0.05 SD in ELA (~43 and ~36 additional learning days). Benefits were disproportionately large for Black students, English learners, and socioeconomically disadvantaged students.

---

### 141. The State of the Teacher Workforce

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/state-of-teacher-workforce-interactive-0"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, k-12, learning-engineering, lpi]
```

Learning Policy Institute interactive report (updated July 2024) providing state-by-state analysis of teacher workforce conditions across all 50 U.S. states. Assigns each state a teaching attractiveness rating (1–5) measuring compensation, working conditions, and retention factors, and a teacher equity rating (1–5) assessing equitable distribution of qualified educators across demographic groups. Draws from national datasets for 2017–18 through 2022–23 academic years.

---

### 142. How People Learn: Brain, Mind, Experience, and School (Expanded Edition)

```yaml
url: "https://www.nationalacademies.org/read/9853/chapter/"
type: report
source: "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [learning-engineering, nap, k-12]
```

National Research Council expanded edition report (2000) edited by Bransford, Brown, and Cocking synthesizing research on learning science across cognitive, developmental, and neuroscientific perspectives. Bridges theoretical findings with classroom practice by examining how expertise develops, how knowledge transfers, and how children acquire understanding. Provides evidence-based guidance for designing effective learning environments across history, mathematics, and science disciplines. Free online reading via National Academies Press.

---

### 143. How People Learn II: Learners, Contexts, and Cultures

```yaml
url: "https://www.nationalacademies.org/publications/24783"
type: report
source: "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [learning-engineering, nap, k-12, higher-ed]
```

National Academies consensus report (2018) chaired by Cora B. Marrett updating the influential 2000 How People Learn volume. Synthesizes findings from cognitive neuroscience, educational research, and learning technologies with emphasis on sociocultural and environmental factors shaping learning across the lifespan. Addresses learning from infancy through adulthood in both formal and informal settings. Free online reading via National Academies Press.

---

### 144. CASEL SEL Framework

```yaml
url: "https://casel.org/fundamentals-of-sel/what-is-the-casel-framework/"
type: framework
source: "Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning (CASEL)"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [sel, learning-engineering, casel, k-12, inclusive-design]
```

CASEL framework (updated 2020) defining the five core social-emotional competencies — self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationship skills, and responsible decision-making — and their implementation across four settings: classrooms, schools, families and caregivers, and communities. The 2020 update strengthened the framework's equity orientation, emphasizing SEL's potential in promoting educational equity and excellence and its adaptability to local cultural contexts.

---

### 145. Engaging First: Supporting Young Learners Through Family Engagement

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/resource/young-learners-family-engagement"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, learning-engineering, edtrust]
```

EdTrust research brief by Ivy Morgan (2024) examining family-school partnerships for early elementary learners based on nationally representative surveys of 600 parents/caregivers and 300 teachers of first and second graders during 2022–23. Finds broadly positive communication overall but significant gaps: only ~two-thirds of parents had access to parent-teacher conferences; low-income and multilingual families faced greater barriers including unwelcoming school staff. Part of EdTrust's broader SEAD research program.

---

### 146. Making Assessment Reports More Meaningful for Students & Families

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/resource/assessment-reports-family-engagement"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, learning-engineering, edtrust]
```

EdTrust report by Munyan-Penney and Levitan (2024) providing guidance for schools on improving communication of federally required annual assessment results to students and families. Advocates for integrating score reports into existing family communications, identifies best practices for accessible report design, and offers policy recommendations for advocates and state leaders to improve assessment reporting at scale.

---

### 147. How Student, Family, and Community Engagement Impacts Students' SEAD

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/resource/student-family-community-engagement-sead"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [sel, k-12, learning-engineering, edtrust]
```

EdTrust report by Duchesneau and Wriston (2024) examining how school, family, and community engagement supports students' social, emotional, and academic development (SEAD). Identifies obstacles to meaningful engagement and provides policy recommendations for federal, state, and local decision-makers. Part of EdTrust's broader research on whole-child development and the role of family-school partnerships in student wellbeing and academic outcomes.

---

### 148. Funding Gaps 2018

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/resource/funding-gaps-2018/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, edtrust]
```

EdTrust report by Morgan and Amerikaner (2018) documenting school funding disparities across U.S. states and districts. Finds districts serving predominantly students of color receive ~$1,800 (13%) less per student in state and local funding than majority-white districts; high-poverty districts receive ~$1,000 (7%) less than low-poverty districts. Gap widens to $2,000 per student (16%) when adjusted for additional costs of supporting low-income students. Documents state-by-state variation and highlights states achieving more equitable funding.

---

### 149. Teacher Turnover in the United States: Who Moves, Who Leaves, and Why

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/teacher-turnover-united-states-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, k-12, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This comprehensive analysis examines why approximately 1 in 7 public school teachers annually leave their schools or exit the profession, using national data from 2020–22. The report finds that turnover rates have increased roughly 27% since the early 1990s, with disparities concentrated in schools serving predominantly low-income students and students of color. Three primary factors significantly influence retention: teacher salaries, effective school leadership, and overall job satisfaction tied to classroom autonomy and collegial support.

---

### 150. Supporting and Sustaining a Diverse Teacher Workforce

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/diverse-teacher-workforce-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, k-12, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This report examines why teachers of color leave the profession at higher rates than their white counterparts and identifies systemic barriers to their retention. Using national survey data from 2017–2021, researchers found that teachers of color face less access to comprehensive preparation, competitive salaries, mentoring support, and positive working environments. The study emphasizes that while recruiting more educators of color matters, retention through improved conditions and support systems is equally critical to building a stable, diverse teaching force.

---

### 151. Untangling the Evidence on Preschool Effectiveness: Insights for Policymakers

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/untangling-evidence-preschool-effectiveness-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, prekindergarten, meta-analysis, lpi]
```

This report synthesizes rigorous evaluations of 21 public preschool programs to clarify conflicting findings about whether quality early childhood education produces meaningful gains for students. The research demonstrates that high-quality preschool leaves children better prepared for school, especially in academic skill development, with benefits extending into adulthood through higher graduation rates and earnings. The report identifies six essential quality elements—including small class sizes, well-trained teachers, developmentally appropriate curricula, and family engagement—and notes that effective programs generate up to $17 in social returns for every dollar invested.

---

### 152. Community Schools as an Effective School Improvement Strategy: A Review of the Evidence

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/community-schools-effective-school-improvement-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, meta-analysis, student-belonging, sel, lpi]
```

This comprehensive report synthesizes findings from 143 rigorous research studies examining how community schools impact student and school outcomes, identifying four core pillars of effective implementation: integrated student supports, expanded learning time, family and community engagement, and collaborative leadership. The study demonstrates that community schools help disadvantaged students overcome barriers to learning by integrating academic instruction with health services, youth development, and community partnerships. The evidence base meets federal requirements under the Every Student Succeeds Act, providing policymakers with research-backed guidance for implementing community schools as an equitable school improvement strategy.

---

### 153. Coaching at Scale: A Strategy for Strengthening the Early Learning Workforce

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/coaching-at-scale-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, coaching, professional-development, lpi]
```

This report examines five early childhood coaching systems across Alabama, Washington, and California to understand how comprehensive coaching can be implemented at scale to improve educator practice and program quality. The research identifies that effective coaching relies on strong partnerships, individualized feedback, focused observations, and well-qualified coaches with expertise in child development and adult learning. The report provides policymakers with eight actionable recommendations, including dedicated funding for coaching, establishing coach qualification standards, and developing regional professional development networks to sustain systemic improvements across the early learning workforce.

---

### 154. Reducing Chronic Absenteeism: Lessons from Community Schools

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/chronic-absenteeism-community-schools-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, attendance, student-belonging, lpi]
```

This report examines how four California community schools successfully reduced chronic absenteeism following the pandemic, when national rates had nearly doubled to 28% by 2022. Key strategies included strengthening family engagement through outreach and home visits, building student connections through mentorship, systematically tracking attendance data, implementing tiered support systems, and leveraging community partnerships to address underlying barriers like housing and mental health needs. The findings emphasize that proactive and preventive approaches combined with trusting relationships prove more effective than punitive measures in addressing attendance challenges.

---

### 155. Strengthening the Educator Pipeline Through Service Scholarships: California's Golden State Teacher Grant Program

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/ca-golden-state-teacher-grant-program-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, k-12, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This report evaluates California's Golden State Teacher Grant Program, which has distributed over $570 million since 2020–21 to help aspiring teachers afford preparation costs in exchange for committing to teach in high-need schools for two years, supporting more than 28,000 candidates and likely contributing to a 23% increase in California-prepared teachers earning credentials. Nearly 75% of survey respondents cited the grant as influential in their decision to pursue teaching, with particularly strong impact among candidates of color and career changers. The report recommends stable long-term funding benchmarked to cover significant portions of preparation costs, improved implementation support, and better data collection systems to track long-term outcomes.

---

### 156. High-Quality Early Childhood Assessment: Learning From States' Use of Kindergarten Entry Assessments

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/high-quality-kindergarten-entry-assessments-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, formative-assessment, prekindergarten, lpi]
```

This report examines kindergarten entry assessments (KEAs) across states, noting that as of 2021, 38 states had adopted KEAs—a fivefold increase over a decade—though concerns arose about inappropriate practices like overly scripted assessments or narrow skill-focused measures that exclude essential developmental domains. The report emphasizes that high-quality KEA content aligns with developmentally appropriate kindergarten standards and measures multiple domains including social-emotional, cognitive, language, and physical development in culturally relevant ways. It recommends states select authentic assessments, build systems supporting teacher instruction and family engagement, and use data to strengthen learning systems rather than evaluate individual providers.

---

### 157. The Building Blocks of High-Quality Early Childhood Education Programs

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/building-blocks-high-quality-early-childhood-education-programs"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, prekindergarten, professional-development, english-learners, lpi]
```

This policy brief identifies nine essential components that characterize effective pre-K programs, grounded in research demonstrating that high-quality early education generates approximately $7 in returns for every dollar invested. The report emphasizes that quality matters tremendously—particularly for low-income children and English learners—and establishes benchmarks including well-qualified teachers with bachelor's degrees in early childhood education, ongoing coaching and mentoring, support for diverse learners, meaningful family partnerships, small class sizes, and robust quality rating systems. These interconnected elements work together to foster the meaningful teacher-child interactions that research shows are fundamental to producing measurable gains in academic achievement and social-emotional development.

---

### 158. Community Schools Impact on Student Outcomes: Evidence From California

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/ca-community-schools-impact-student-outcomes-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, attendance, student-belonging, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This report evaluates California's $4.1 billion Community Schools Partnership Program, the nation's largest state-level community schools initiative launched in 2021, using a matched difference-in-differences methodology comparing 458 CCSPP schools to similar non-recipient schools from 2018–19 to 2023–24. Researchers found that community school approaches led to a meaningful reduction in chronic absence averaging 30% greater than comparison schools, along with notable improvements in suspension rates and test scores. The most substantial gains benefited historically underserved students including Black students, English learners, and economically disadvantaged youth.

---

### 159. Teacher Residencies in Texas: Supporting Successful Implementation

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/texas-teacher-residencies-implementation-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, k-12, coaching, lpi]
```

This report examines Texas's High-Quality, Sustainable Residencies Program, which funded over 85 teacher residency programs from 2021–2024 using COVID-19 relief money and provided stipends for more than 2,000 residents. The research identifies essential program features—including full-year classroom placements, $20,000 resident stipends, mentor teachers, and co-teaching models—as critical to successful implementation, while highlighting how strategic staffing models help districts sustain paid residencies beyond grant funding. The report concludes with six policy recommendations including requirements for research-based residency features, support for mentor teacher pipelines, and targeted funding for rural and high-poverty districts facing acute teacher shortages.

---

### 160. Diversifying the Teaching Profession: How to Recruit and Retain Teachers of Color

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/diversifying-teaching-profession-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, k-12, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This report examines barriers that impede teachers of color from entering and remaining in the profession, while documenting how such teachers significantly benefit student outcomes. The research reveals that while recruitment has increased, attrition rates remain high, creating persistent supply-demand gaps particularly among Black, Native American, and Latinx educators. The study identifies three primary intervention areas: high-retention pathways into teaching through scholarships and residencies; hiring strategies that include diverse committees and early recruitment; and improving school conditions through better leadership preparation.

---

### 161. Design Principles for Teacher Preparation: Enacting the Science of Learning and Development

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/sold-design-principles-teacher-preparation-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [professional-development, k-12, sel, lpi]
```

This report presents five design principles for teacher preparation programs that incorporate findings from neuroscience, developmental psychology, and learning sciences to help future educators support whole child development. The framework emphasizes that teacher candidates must themselves experience the very kinds of teaching strategies they are expected to develop for their pupils, ensuring teachers can authentically model approaches like inquiry-based learning and restorative practices. The principles address curriculum grounded in learning science, equitable educator mindsets, experiential learning opportunities, pedagogical alignment between university coursework and school placements, and the creation of supportive professional learning communities.

---

### 162. State Preschool in a Mixed Delivery System: Lessons From Five States

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/state-preschool-mixed-delivery-system-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, prekindergarten, professional-development, lpi]
```

This report examines how five states—Alabama, Michigan, New Jersey, New York, and West Virginia—operate public preschool programs across diverse provider settings including school districts and community-based organizations. The study identifies key policy decisions regarding governance, quality standards, teacher compensation, and enrollment coordination that enable states to maintain consistent educational quality while serving children through multiple delivery channels. The authors recommend that states establish uniform quality benchmarks across all settings, remove barriers to non-LEA participation, provide sustained professional development, ensure competitive teacher compensation, implement coordinated enrollment systems, and collect demographic data to identify access disparities.

---

### 163. Developing Assessment Systems That Support Teaching and Learning: Recommendations for Federal Support

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/developing-assessment-systems-federal-support-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, ai-policy, lpi]
```

This report examines how the federal government can support states in transforming their assessment systems beyond traditional end-of-year multiple-choice tests, noting that more than 20 states are involved in efforts to transform one or more aspects of their assessment systems but face substantial barriers to federal approval. The authors identify three key recommendations for federal executive action: updating the Department of Education's peer review process to align with ESSA's encouragement of innovative assessments, strengthening the Innovative Assessment Demonstration Authority program to reduce burdensome requirements, and expanding funding pathways to incentivize state-level innovation in assessment design.

---

### 164. School Funding Effectiveness: Evidence From California's Local Control Funding Formula

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/school-funding-effectiveness-ca-lcff-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This comprehensive study examines how California's 2013 Local Control Funding Formula—which directed $18 billion in increased state support to high-need districts over eight years—affected student outcomes across the state's 1,000 districts and 10,000 schools. Tracking 6.2 million K-12 students from 1995–96 through 2018–19, the research finds that a $1,000 per-pupil spending increase yielded full grade-level improvements in math and reading after three years, boosted high school graduation rates by 8.2 percentage points, and decreased suspensions and expulsions particularly for Black students. Instructional spending—class size reduction, teacher salary increases, and reduced turnover—accounted for roughly 84% of the variation in funding effectiveness.

---

### 165. Lost Opportunities: How Disparate School Discipline Continues to Drive Differences in the Opportunity to Learn

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/crdc-school-discipline-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, school-discipline, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This report examines how out-of-school suspensions and other exclusionary discipline practices create substantial learning loss, with particularly severe impacts on students of color, secondary students, and those with disabilities. During the 2015–16 school year alone, the nation lost approximately 11.4 million instructional days due to suspensions—the equivalent of 62,596 years of lost learning. The research documents widespread racial disparities in discipline outcomes and significant failures by districts to report school-policing data as required by federal law.

---

### 166. Long-Term English Learners in California

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/ca-long-term-english-learners-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, english-learners, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This report examines approximately 330,000 California students classified as English learners for 7 or more years, analyzing their demographics, school contexts, and academic outcomes compared to peers. The research reveals that long-term English learners are disproportionately boys (56%), economically disadvantaged (89%), and have higher special education needs (28%), while attending schools with fewer resources and lower graduation rates. Key recommendations include ensuring adequate schooling resources and whole-child supports, addressing teacher shortages in high-poverty schools, better identifying early learning needs for students with disabilities, and conducting further research on graduation barriers for this vulnerable population.

---

### 167. Investing for Student Success: Lessons From State School Finance Reforms

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/investing-student-success-school-finance-reforms-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This report examines how U.S. public schools are inequitably funded through reliance on local property taxes and analyzes reform efforts in Connecticut, Massachusetts, New Jersey, and North Carolina that successfully improved educational resources and outcomes. The research demonstrates that when more money is spent on education, especially for students from low-income families, achievement and graduation rates improve alongside life outcomes like employment and wages. The report recommends federal and state actions—including equalizing funding allocations, ensuring qualified teachers reach high-poverty schools, and investing in quality preschool—as essential steps toward creating equitable learning opportunities for all students.

---

### 168. Advancing Integration and Equity Through Magnet Schools

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/advancing-integration-equity-magnet-schools-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, school-discipline, student-belonging, lpi]
```

This report examines how magnet schools—public schools offering specialized programs to attract diverse student populations—can combat the alarming resegregation occurring in U.S. schools. The authors identify key components for success, including "first door" strategies like intentional family outreach and inclusive enrollment, plus "second door" approaches such as culturally responsive curriculum and restorative discipline practices. The report provides policy recommendations at federal, state, district, and school levels to help create and sustain magnet schools that achieve both integration and positive academic outcomes for all students.

---

### 169. Instructionally Relevant Assessment Systems: What is the Role of Performance Assessments?

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/instructionally-relevant-assessment-systems-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, professional-development, lpi]
```

This report examines how performance assessments—tasks requiring human scoring like constructed responses and essays—can transform state assessment systems to prioritize positive instructional impact alongside accountability functions. The author argues that because teachers inevitably take cues from state-level assessments to shape their instruction, assessment designers should intentionally leverage this influence by incorporating authentic performance tasks that signal high-quality teaching practices. The report provides system leaders with five strategic recommendations for balancing performance assessments with other instruments, emphasizing that well-designed tasks can generate reliable comparable scores while simultaneously improving what students learn and how teachers teach.

---

### 170. The Promise of Performance Assessments: Innovations in High School Learning and Higher Education Admissions

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/promise-performance-assessments-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, higher-ed, college-access, lpi]
```

This report examines how schools and colleges are adopting performance-based assessments—such as portfolios, capstone projects, and senior defenses—to evaluate deeper learning skills needed for college and career success. The authors document that more than 900 colleges have made standardized tests optional and are increasingly interested in reviewing broader student work samples for admission decisions. A key finding is that these assessments show particular promise for better reflecting the achievements and potential of historically underserved students, potentially addressing longstanding disparities in standardized testing outcomes across racial and socioeconomic lines.

---

### 171. Promising Models for Preparing a Diverse, High-Quality Early Childhood Workforce

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/preparing-diverse-high-quality-early-childhood-workforce-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, professional-development, coaching, lpi]
```

This report examines innovative pathways for recruiting and preparing early childhood educators with a focus on creating accessible routes for diverse candidates, identifying three successful California programs that demonstrate how to support educators from underrepresented backgrounds through affordable, hands-on preparation models. The analysis reveals significant barriers early educators face when pursuing higher education, notably that low wages often make higher education costs prohibitively expensive. The report recommends that states invest in faculty development, clinical experiences, clear transfer pathways between community and four-year colleges, robust financial assistance, and coherent credential standards to ensure all young children have qualified teachers reflecting their communities' diversity.

---

### 172. Community Schools in Lynwood Unified: Building Capacity for Districtwide Implementation

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/ca-community-schools-lynwood-unified-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, attendance, professional-development, lpi]
```

This report examines how Lynwood Unified School District successfully scaled community schools across all 17 campuses by strategically developing community school coordinators as implementation leaders, leveraging $24 million in state funding to expand from a single pilot school to a districtwide initiative. The district achieved measurable improvements including a one-third reduction in chronic absence, enhanced academic performance, and a 15.5% decline in suspensions within two years. The research highlights five critical implementation strategies including data-driven needs assessments, structured professional learning communities, and coaching networks that positioned coordinators as essential change agents rather than peripheral support roles.

---

### 173. State Strategies for Investing in Community Schools

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/state-strategies-investing-in-community-schools-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, student-belonging, sel, lpi]
```

This report examines how states are funding and supporting community schools by identifying three primary approaches: ongoing funding through school financing formulas (Maryland, New York), competitive grant programs (California, Illinois, New Mexico, Vermont), and capacity-building supports like certification processes (Florida, Georgia). State investments range from $3.4 million to $4.1 billion, with most states prioritizing schools serving high-poverty populations. Key findings show states are increasingly investing in evidence-based strategies including integrated student supports, expanded learning time, family engagement, and collaborative leadership structures that align with trauma-informed practices.

---

### 174. The Road to High-Quality Early Learning: Lessons From the States

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/road-high-quality-early-learning-lessons-states"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [early-childhood, prekindergarten, professional-development, coaching, lpi]
```

This report examines how four states—Michigan, West Virginia, Washington, and North Carolina—have built high-quality early education systems, drawing on policy reviews, program observations, and interviews with 159 stakeholders. The analysis identifies five overarching strategies these states employ: establishing quality standards and improvement systems, investing in educator development, coordinating birth-through-grade-3 programs, combining multiple funding sources, and building broad-based coalitions. The report provides policymakers with actionable insights on defining and using state quality standards, linking funding to ratings, investing in teacher training and coaching, and fostering cross-agency coordination to create seamless educational experiences for young learners.

---


### 175. Opportunities Denied: High-Achieving Black and Latino Students Lack Access to Advanced Math

```yaml

type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, math-education, english-learners, edtrust]
```

High-achieving Black and Latino students and students from low-income backgrounds who pass Algebra I in eighth grade still enroll in advanced high school math at lower rates than their peers. Developed jointly with Just Equations, this brief documents enrollment disparities and identifies differences in teacher and counselor practices as contributing factors to these access gaps.

```yaml
source: "The Education Trust"

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, sel, student-belonging, edtrust]
```

Schools often narrowly focus on changing student behavior rather than building relationships and supportive learning environments, disproportionately affecting students of color and low-income students. This framework guides school and district leaders to shift attention from "fixing kids" to examining adult beliefs, school policies, and district practices that create equitable learning environments, ensuring all students experience belonging, challenging opportunities, and appropriate supports.

### 177. Hard Truths: Why Only Race-Conscious Policies Can Fix Racism in Higher Education

```yaml

type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01

```

This brief addresses misconceptions about affirmative action by demonstrating that Black and Latino students remain underrepresented at selective colleges and public universities across most states. The report argues that addressing racial inequities in higher education requires explicit race-conscious policy interventions, as income-based alternatives have failed to close opportunity gaps for students of color.

---

### 178. If You Listen, We Will Stay: Why Teachers of Color Leave and How to Disrupt Teacher Turnover

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/if-you-listen-we-will-stay/"

source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true

date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, professional-development, edtrust]
```

Released jointly by The Education Trust and Teach Plus, this research examines why educators of color leave the teaching profession at higher rates than their peers despite representing less than 20% of the nation's teaching force. Drawing on focus groups with Black and Latino teachers and case studies of schools successfully retaining faculty of color, the report identifies five key barriers teachers face and five actions effective schools employ to support diverse educators.

This analysis examines college degree completion rates among Black adults aged 25–64 across the United States and individual states. The report contends that the nation's colleges and universities are not adequately supporting Black and Latino student success, and that policymakers must prioritize higher education access and completion for historically underserved populations to meet national workforce demands and promote economic growth.

### 180. Inequities in Advanced Coursework

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/inequities-in-advanced-coursework/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, college-access, edtrust]
```

This report examines barriers preventing Black and Latino students from accessing AP, IB, and dual enrollment programs, finding that while these students succeed when given opportunities, they remain underrepresented due to three compounding factors: schools serving predominantly students of color offering fewer advanced courses, lower overall enrollment in those courses, and racially diverse schools actively denying access. The report provides recommendations for state and district leaders to expand access and improve equity in advanced coursework enrollment.

---

### 181. 'Segregation Forever?': The Continued Underrepresentation of Black Undergraduates at the Nation's 122 Most Selective Private Colleges and Universities

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/segregation-forever-the-continued-underrepresentation-of-black-undergraduates-at-the-nations-122-most-selective-private-colleges-and-universities/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, longitudinal, edtrust]
```

EdTrust examined Black student enrollment at 122 selective private colleges and universities in 2000 and 2020, finding that despite nearly 74% of these institutions increasing Black enrollment, 71% still received failing grades for Black student access in 2020. The research reveals that gains were minimal and that elite institutions remain largely inaccessible to Black first-time undergraduates relative to state population demographics.

---

### 183. How School Discipline Impacts Students' Social, Emotional, and Academic Development (SEAD)


This brief examines how school discipline policies affect student development, highlighting that harmful practices like corporal punishment, suspensions, and expulsions disproportionately impact students of color and those with disabilities. The report advocates for evidence-based approaches such as restorative justice and emphasizes the importance of implementing discipline policies through a racial equity lens to create safe, supportive learning environments.

### 184. 5 Things for Advocates to Know About Chronic Absenteeism

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/5-things-for-advocates-to-know-about-chronic-absenteeism/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, attendance, edtrust]
```

This brief documents the rising epidemic of chronic school absence, noting that 16 million students nationally are now chronically absent — missing 10% or more of school days — with disproportionate impact on low-income students, students of color, multilingual learners, and students with disabilities. The report advocates for evidence-based approaches addressing root causes rather than punitive measures, and highlights the importance of prevention, early intervention, and authentic partnerships with families and communities.

### 185. How Mental Health Supports Impact Social, Emotional, and Academic Development (SEAD)


This brief examines how school-based mental health services support student success, highlighting significant disparities in access for students of color, low-income students, and students with disabilities. The authors present CDC data showing alarming mental health challenges among high school students and emphasize that district leaders must implement mental health support services rooted in a race-based lens to address these inequities.

### 186. Future of Assessments: Centering Equity and the Lived Experiences of Students, Families, and Educators

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/future-of-assessments-centering-equity-and-the-lived-experiences-of-students-families-and-educators/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, formative-assessment, qualitative-research, edtrust]
```

This report examines statewide assessment practices through focus groups with students, families, educators, and administrators, revealing that stakeholders want assessment data from multiple sources, believe assessments are just one measure of college readiness, and seek timely and actionable results alongside improved resource allocation to underserved schools. The report proposes four equity pillars to guide future federal education policy and assessment design.

---

### 187. Holding Students Back: An Inequitable and Ineffective Response to Unfinished Learning

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/holding-students-back-an-inequitable-and-ineffective-response-to-unfinished-learning/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, response-to-intervention, edtrust]
```

This factsheet examines grade retention as a response to pandemic-related learning loss, arguing that while retention may show short-term test score improvements, research demonstrates it produces negative long-term academic, social, and emotional outcomes — particularly for Black, Latino, and Native students. The analysis recommends evidence-based alternatives including targeted tutoring, expanded learning time, and strong teacher-student relationships.

---

### 188. How Affordable Are Public Colleges in Your State? (Affordability Gap)

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/affordability-gap/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, edtrust]
```

This report examines the affordability gap that low-income students face at public colleges across all states, finding that after accounting for grants, scholarships, and part-time work, students from low-income backgrounds still face thousands of dollars in uncovered annual costs. In nearly every state the affordability gap at four-year institutions exceeds $3,000, with the national average around $6,500.

---

### 189. The Future of Statewide Summative Assessments

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/the-future-of-statewide-summative-assessments/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, formative-assessment, edtrust]
```

EdTrust identifies statewide summative assessments as an essential tool to achieve education equity by providing comparable data across students and schools. The organization presents four equity pillars for improving assessments — ensuring consistent expectations, encouraging inclusive assessments, providing timely results, and making results meaningful — offering advocates and policymakers recommendations for enhancing assessment systems to better serve students, families, and educators.

---

### 190. Parent PLUS Loans Are a Double-Edged Sword for Black Borrowers

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/parent-plus-loans-are-a-double-edged-sword-for-black-borrowers/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, edtrust]
```

This brief examines how Black parents increasingly rely on Parent PLUS loans to fund their children's college education, despite these loans carrying higher interest rates and less favorable terms than federal student loans. The report reveals that structural racism, including racial wage and wealth gaps, forces Black families to borrow heavily, and that Black parent borrowers struggle significantly with repayment — often deferring payments and drawing from retirement accounts at higher rates than White borrowers, creating a multigenerational toll of debt.

---

### 191. Income-Driven Repayment Plans Fail Black Borrowers

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/income-driven-repayment-plans-fail-black-borrowers/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, qualitative-research, edtrust]
```

Drawing on qualitative data from EdTrust's National Black Student Debt Study, this brief examines how federal income-driven repayment plans are ineffective at reducing debt for Black borrowers. The authors argue that even recent administrative improvements do not address underlying college affordability issues or help future students, and recommend broad debt cancellation plus creation of a new repayment plan with more favorable terms.

---

### 192. For Student Parents, The Biggest Hurdles to a Higher Education Are Costs and Finding Child Care

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/for-student-parents-the-biggest-hurdles-to-a-higher-education-are-costs-and-finding-child-care/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, dropout-prevention, edtrust]
```

This report examines the financial barriers facing student parents, finding that there is no state in which a student parent can work 10 hours per week at minimum wage and afford both tuition and child care at a public college. Student parents face out-of-pocket costs that are 2 to 5 times higher than peers without children, requiring an average of 52 hours of weekly work per week to cover expenses.

---

### 193. Building a Diverse & Strong Educator Workforce for Students

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/building-a-diverse-strong-educator-workforce-for-students/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, professional-development, edtrust]
```

This resource highlights a significant disparity in educator demographics: students of color make up more than 50% of the K-12 public school student population while teachers of color represent less than 20% of educators. Students from underserved backgrounds are four times more likely to attend schools with larger percentages of uncertified and inexperienced teachers, contributing to disparities in discipline rates, graduation outcomes, and educational access.

---

### 194. Access Granted: School Funding Between Schools in Districts

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/access-granted-school-funding-between-schools-in-districts/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, edtrust]
```

EdTrust analyzed newly available school-by-school spending data required by the Every Student Succeeds Act to examine funding equity within districts. The analysis reveals that in 97% of districts included, state and local spending was flat or regressive for all student groups examined, with very few students of color, low-income students, or English learners attending schools where funding is allocated to meet their additional needs.

---

### 195. Reassessing ESSA Implementation: An Equity Analysis of School Accountability Systems

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/reassessing-essa-implementation-an-equity-analysis/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, edtrust]
```

EdTrust examined how states implement federal school accountability requirements under the Every Student Succeeds Act, finding that states have a mixed record of centering equity in school ratings. The analysis identifies significant gaps in how states identify low-performing schools and support improvement efforts, and reveals that while some states show promising practices, most have failed to connect school identification processes to robust support systems that would meaningfully improve outcomes for historically underserved students.

---

### 196. Jim Crow Debt: How Black Borrowers Experience Student Loans

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/jim-crow-debt/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, qualitative-research, edtrust]
```

This report presents findings from the National Black Student Loan Debt Study, based on a survey of nearly 1,300 Black borrowers and interviews with 100 participants. Key findings reveal that Black borrowers view student loans as problematic rather than beneficial, experience income-driven repayment plans as lifetime debt sentences, and overwhelmingly support complete government debt forgiveness as a racial justice solution, with the research connecting these outcomes to systemic racism and labor market stratification.

---

### 197. Creating Positive College Campus Racial Climates for Students of Color

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/college-campus-racial-climates-2/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, student-belonging, qualitative-research, edtrust]
```

EdTrust interviewed 32 Black, Latino, and Asian American students at predominantly White institutions to understand how campus racial climate affects their experiences. The research identified key challenges including underrepresentation of students and faculty of color, limited institutional support, unclear reporting procedures for discrimination incidents, and insufficient mental health resources tailored to students of color.

---

### 198. How Student Debt Harms Black Borrowers' Mental Health

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/how-student-debt-harms-black-borrowers-mental-health/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, student-belonging, edtrust]
```

Drawing on the National Black Student Debt Study, this brief examines the intersection of student debt and mental health among Black borrowers, finding that 64% of survey participants reported that student debt negatively impacted their mental health. The report documents how Black borrowers face disproportionate challenges with student loans due to structural racism and fewer generational resources, establishing that the psychological toll of debt can be as severe as its financial consequences.

---

### 199. How Black Women Experience Student Debt

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/how-black-women-experience-student-debt/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, edtrust]
```

This brief examines how Black women are disproportionately affected by the student debt crisis due to intersecting discrimination and systemic inequities. Using federal data and the National Black Student Debt Study, the report demonstrates that Black women borrow more for college while earning less, making repayment significantly more challenging than for other groups.

---

### 200. Making College Affordable

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/making-college-affordable/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [higher-ed, college-access, edtrust]
```

EdTrust advocates for reducing college costs and student debt through policy initiatives focused on highlighting affordability gaps in higher education, promoting free college programs, doubling Pell Grants, and addressing the disproportionate student debt burden faced by Black borrowers. The organization's goal is enabling students to earn degrees without it turning into a debt sentence, and this hub page links to related research, data tools, and advocacy resources on college affordability.

---

### 201. Evidence for ESSA: Reading Partners

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/reading-partners/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, coaching]
```

Reading Partners is a one-to-one tutoring program delivered by trained community volunteers who work with struggling readers in grades 2–5 for two 45-minute sessions per week. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one MDRC randomized controlled trial (n=1,166) showing students gained significantly more on SAT-10 comprehension and other outcomes (effect size +0.10) compared to peers, two-thirds of whom received alternative literacy services.

---

### 202. Evidence for ESSA: Success for All

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/success-for-all-whole-class/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, professional-development, coaching]
```

Success for All is a comprehensive K–5 school reform program combining phonics instruction, cooperative learning, small-group tutoring, and frequent regrouping for high-poverty schools. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on six U.S. studies (n=8,849 students across 207 schools) including two RCTs, with an average effect size of +0.29 and particularly robust effects on foundational reading skills and for lower-achieving students.

---

### 203. Evidence for ESSA: Reading Recovery

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/reading-recovery/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, coaching]
```

Reading Recovery targets first-graders in the lowest 20–30% of their cohort, delivering daily 30-minute one-to-one lessons from specially trained teachers covering fluency, decoding, phonemic awareness, and guided reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four qualifying studies (n=7,756 students) with an average effect size of +0.43 on standardized reading assessments including ITBS, Woodcock, and Gates measures.

---

### 204. Evidence for ESSA: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) — Reading

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/peer-assisted-learning-strategies-pals-reading-elementary-whole-class/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12]
```

PALS is a peer-tutoring approach where first-grade student pairs alternate teacher and learner roles, working through a structured sequence of phonemic awareness, phonics, sound blending, and passage reading with error correction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=1,501 students) with an average effect size of +0.23 on Woodcock reading scales.

---

### 205. Evidence for ESSA: Enhanced Core Reading Instruction (ECRI)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/enhanced-core-reading-instruction-ecri/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, professional-development, response-to-intervention]
```

ECRI enhances Tier 2 supplemental reading instruction for struggling first graders by providing teacher professional development and small-group sessions (3–5 students) targeting phonemic awareness, decoding, fluency, and irregular word reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=811 students in Oregon and Massachusetts) with an average effect size of +0.24 on Woodcock-Johnson and SAT-10 measures.

---

### 206. Evidence for ESSA: Reading Corps

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/reading-corps/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, coaching]
```

Reading Corps is an AmeriCorps program that places trained literacy tutors in K–3 classrooms to deliver 20-minute daily supplemental instruction using research-based protocols. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=798 students in Minnesota and Wisconsin) with an average effect size of +0.36, with particularly strong gains in Letter Sound Fluency (+0.74) and Nonsense Word Fluency (+0.62) for K–1 students.

---

### 207. Evidence for ESSA: Targeted Reading Instruction (TRI)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/targeted-reading-instruction-tri/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, coaching, computer-assisted-learning]
```

TRI is a one-to-one tutoring intervention that uses webcam technology to train educators to deliver 15-minute daily individualized reading sessions for struggling K–1 readers, covering oral language, decoding, writing, comprehension, vocabulary, and fluency. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=471 students) with an average effect size of +0.51 on letter-word identification, word attack, and passage comprehension measures.

---

### 208. Evidence for ESSA: Sound Partners — Struggling Readers

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/sound-partners-struggling-readers/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, coaching]
```

Sound Partners is a one-on-one paraprofessional tutoring intervention for kindergarten and first-grade struggling readers, providing 30 minutes of daily instruction four days per week for 18–20 weeks in phonics, phonemic awareness, sight words, and decodable text reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two qualifying studies (n=488 students) with an average effect size of +0.58, with sustained gains confirmed at two-year follow-up assessments.

---

### 209. Evidence for ESSA: QuickReads — Struggling Readers

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/quickreads-struggling-readers/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, coaching]
```

QuickReads is a supplemental tutoring program for struggling readers in grades 2–5 that pairs students for 30-minute daily sessions four days per week over approximately 20 weeks, emphasizing repeated reading, phonics, and comprehension. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=483 students) with an average effect size of +0.21 on GORT and Woodcock reading assessments.

---

### 210. Evidence for ESSA: Air Reading

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/air-reading/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, coaching, computer-assisted-learning]
```

Air Reading is a virtual tutoring program for grades 1–6 that delivers explicit, skills-based reading instruction in small groups of up to four students meeting four times weekly for 30 minutes, with consistent tutors using diagnostic assessment to guide individualized instruction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=740 students in Texas and Louisiana) with a weighted average effect size of +0.20 across NWEA MAP, DIBELS, LEAP, and STAAR assessments.

---

### 211. Evidence for ESSA: Building Assets Reducing Risks (BARR) — Reading

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/building-assets-reducing-risks-barr-reading/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, professional-development]
```

BARR is a training and coaching model for K–12 schools built around structured teacher team meetings, data-driven student support, community engagement, and professional development to improve student outcomes. Evidence for ESSA rates the reading component Strong based on three randomized studies (n=9,398 students) with an average effect size of +0.08, with stronger impacts of +0.21 for struggling readers in high school settings.

---

### 212. Evidence for ESSA: Intelligent Tutoring for the Structure Strategy (ITSS)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/intelligent-tutoring-for-the-structure-strategy-itss-secondary/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [literacy, k-12, intelligent-tutoring, computer-assisted-learning]
```

ITSS is a web-based intelligent tutoring system that teaches nonfiction text comprehension through text structure categorization, delivered in 30–45-minute weekly sessions in language arts classes with paraprofessional support. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one RCT (n=2,489 seventh graders across 108 classrooms in 25 rural/suburban schools) with an effect size of +0.18 on the Gray Silent Reading Test after 6–7 months of use.

---

### 213. Evidence for ESSA: Zearn Math

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/zearn-math/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, computer-assisted-learning, personalized-learning]
```

Zearn Math is a nonprofit adaptive online supplement for K–8 math instruction featuring guided video lessons using a Concrete-to-Pictorial-to-Abstract progression with mastery-based quizzes. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (including one RCT with 10,000+ students in Texas) with an average effect size of +0.09 on NWEA MAP assessments, with strongest results when schools received dedicated implementation support including professional development and coaching.

---

### 214. Evidence for ESSA: ASSISTments

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/assistments/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, computer-assisted-learning, formative-assessment, automated-feedback]
```

ASSISTments is an online homework tool for grades 7–8 that provides students with math assistance during practice while giving teachers real-time assessment data on student progress. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (a Maine study and a North Carolina replication with ~6,000 students) with a combined mean effect size of +0.13 on mathematics assessments.

---

### 215. Evidence for ESSA: DreamBox Math

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/dreambox-learning/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, computer-assisted-learning, personalized-learning, intelligent-tutoring]
```

DreamBox Math is an adaptive game-based online platform that delivers personalized, standards-aligned K–8 math instruction as a supplement to classroom teaching. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=13,320 students in California and North Carolina) with an average effect size of +0.10 on the Number Knowledge Test and state assessments, with strongest impacts for K–2 students.

---

### 216. Evidence for ESSA: ST Math (Spatial Temporal Math)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/st-math-spatial-temporal-math/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, computer-assisted-learning]
```

ST Math is a supplemental visual problem-solving program for grades 3–6 built on the science of how the brain learns, using animation and game-based tasks to develop mathematical understanding without language barriers. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four studies (n=346,248 students) including two cluster-randomized trials in high-poverty schools, with an average effect size of +0.07 on state math assessments.

---

### 217. Evidence for ESSA: Math in Focus

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/math-in-focus/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, professional-development, math-strategies]
```

Math in Focus adapts Singapore's "My Pals Are Here! Maths" curriculum for grades 2–5 using a concrete-to-pictorial-to-abstract progression in carefully scaffolded lessons, supported by extensive professional development including summer training and four full-day sessions during the school year. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four qualifying studies (n=3,444 students) with an average effect size of +0.15 on standardized math measures.

---

### 218. Evidence for ESSA: IXL Math

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/ixl-math/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, computer-assisted-learning, personalized-learning, formative-assessment]
```

IXL Math is a comprehensive personalized teaching and learning platform featuring adaptive skills practice aligned to state standards, video tutorials, diagnostic assessments, and analytics for monitoring progress at district, school, classroom, and individual levels. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one cluster RCT (n=545 elementary students in Michigan) with an effect size of +0.13 on Renaissance Star Math, with particularly strong impacts for Grade 3, Hispanic, special education, and English learner subgroups.

---

### 219. Evidence for ESSA: Saga Math

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/saga-math/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, coaching, computer-assisted-learning]
```

Saga Math is a high-dosage tutoring intervention for grades 9–10 offering traditional (1:2–1:4) or technology-enhanced formats (1:8 alternating direct tutoring with ALEKS computer-assisted learning), delivered three times weekly for 90 minutes total to supplement regular math instruction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=5,396+ predominantly Black and Latinx students in Chicago) with an average effect size of +0.14, plus increased math GPAs, reduced course failures, and persistent gains one to two years post-intervention.

---

### 220. Evidence for ESSA: Math Corps

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/math-corps/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, coaching]
```

Math Corps is a tutoring intervention for grades 4–8 where full-time AmeriCorps tutors provide approximately 25 students with 90 minutes of weekly intervention support in small sessions targeting foundational whole and rational number skills and algebraic reasoning. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=1,164 students) with an average effect size of +0.16 on STAR Math and state assessments.

---

### 221. Evidence for ESSA: OGAP Math Professional Learning System

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/ogap-math-professional-learning-system-the-ongoing-assessment-project/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, professional-development, formative-assessment]
```

OGAP Math is a professional learning system for grades 3–5 teachers that develops mathematics content knowledge and formative assessment skills using research-based learning progressions, enabling teachers to analyze student work and identify strategies and misconceptions in real time. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one two-year RCT (n=3,488 students across 61 urban schools near Philadelphia) with an effect size of +0.20 on Pennsylvania state math assessments.

---

### 222. Evidence for ESSA: Graspable Math — From Here to There (FH2T)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/graspable-math-from-here-to-there-fh2t/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, computer-assisted-learning, math-strategies]
```

From Here to There (FH2T) is a digital math game for grade 7 in which students strengthen algebraic skills by dragging and transforming expressions using valid mathematical operations, practicing distribution, factoring, inverse operations, and order of operations. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=1,119 seventh graders across 10 suburban middle schools) with an effect size of +0.14 on an algebraic understanding assessment after nine 30-minute sessions.

---

### 223. Evidence for ESSA: My Math Academy

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/my-math-academy/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, early-childhood, prekindergarten, computer-assisted-learning, personalized-learning]
```

My Math Academy is a game-based adaptive learning system for pre-K through 2nd grade featuring 30 games with 130+ activities, adaptive scaffolding, embedded assessment, and narrative-driven characters to build foundational number sense and operations. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=1,209 students in Texas and Southern California) with an average effect size of +0.27 on CIRCLE and TEMA-3 math assessments.

---

### 224. Evidence for ESSA: Whole Number Foundations (Roots)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/roots/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, response-to-intervention]
```

Whole Number Foundations is a Tier 2 supplemental intervention for kindergarten and first graders delivered by paraprofessionals in small groups of 1–5, with kindergarteners receiving 50 twenty-minute daily lessons and first graders receiving 60 sessions three to five times weekly targeting procedural fluency and conceptual understanding. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on five studies (n=2,140 students across Oregon, Massachusetts, Texas, and Boston) with an average effect size of +0.19 on SESAT and TEMA-3 measures.

---

### 225. Evidence for ESSA: Number Rockets

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/number-rockets/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, coaching]
```

Number Rockets is a scripted small-group tutoring intervention for first graders delivered by retired and substitute teachers in groups of 2–3 students for 40 minutes daily over 17 weeks, progressing from concrete to abstract mathematical tasks to build foundational number sense. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=994 students below the 38th percentile) with an effect size of +0.34 on TEMA-3 assessments.

---

### 226. Evidence for ESSA: Fraction Face-Off!

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/fraction-face-off/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, coaching, math-strategies]
```

Fraction Face-Off! is a small-group tutoring intervention for at-risk fourth graders that delivers 35-minute daily sports-themed sessions over 12 weeks (36 days) focused on comparing, ordering, placing on number lines, and understanding equivalencies of fractions. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=425 students) with an average effect size of +0.51 on NAEP-derived fraction assessments.

---

### 227. Evidence for ESSA: Building Assets Reducing Risks (BARR) — Math

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/building-assets-reducing-risks-barr-math/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [math-education, k-12, professional-development]
```

BARR is a training and coaching model for K–12 schools that structures teacher team meetings around student relationship data and progress, supplemented by community engagement sessions and professional development, to improve academic outcomes. Evidence for ESSA rates the math component Strong based on three RCTs (n=9,386 students) with an average effect size of +0.09 on NWEA MAP and PSAT/NMSQT math assessments, with a +0.33 effect in one high school cohort.

---

### 228. Evidence for ESSA: Second Step — Social-Emotional Learning

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/second-step-social-emotional-learning/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
tags: [sel, k-12]
```
